text: So this is another in our series of what I would consider advanced robotic lobectomies.
These are lobectomies in patients that have complete fissures. So it's a thick, complete fissure, and you have to be able to handle that.
This seems to cause folks the most challenges as they're learning how to do robotic lobectomies.
This is going to be a left upper lobectomy with an additional challenge, and that is the patient has a patent left internal mammary artery graft.
He had prior bypass surgery The lima was going to the LAD. It was patent. He needed it.
And I think the da Vinci is a great way to do these procedures.
It clearly has advantages over vats for this type of procedure because you can dissect the lima so cleanly away from the left upper lobe.
Tom D'Amico wrote this paper that's cut in here that we actually left a wedge of lung parenchyma attached to the lima to avoid having to dissect it cleanly away from the lobe.

And he said the survival is the same, but intuitively, if you can dissect the whole lobe away from the lemma and you don't have to leave behind any of that pulmonary parenchyma from the lobe harboring the tumor attached to the lemma, that would be preferable. It's not a compromise operation.
And we'll go through the steps of how to do this safely.
So here's my port set up for left upper lobectomy. And I have another video where I talk about this in detail.
Briefly, I use four robotic ports. I use the twelve millimeter ports for the stapler, both anterior and posterior.
The reason is the posterior one is the best angle to take the left upper lobe vein and usually the first branch of the left upper lobe pulmonary artery.

The surgeon places the first port, an eight millimeter camera port, at the junction between the sternum and the xiphoid, aligning it with the sternal notch and positioning it at the seventh intercostal space.
If they've already had a mammary taken down and they've essentially been in the left chest, there may be a lot of adhesions. So use the Be very careful when you put this one in.

The surgeon uses a black plastic obturator with a blunt tip to navigate through the surgical field. In the presence of multiple adhesions, a twelve millimeter port is inserted, employing a VATS technique to carefully dissect and remove adhesions, facilitating the placement of four robotic ports.
The surgeon positions the twelve millimeter assistant port at the junction between the diaphragm and thorax, triangulated between the medial staple port and the camera port.
The surgeon proceeds with the resection, encountering numerous adhesions in the chest cavity. Bipolar cautery is used to carefully lyse these adhesions, separating the tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the camera in arm two while using the Cartier instrument in arm one and the bipolar cautery in arm three, avoiding the lung parenchyma to prevent air leaks.
The surgeon switches the camera to arm three to obtain a more medial view of the anatomy. The Caudillet is positioned in arm two, while the long bipolar instrument is placed in arm four.

I think I got a better look at it. And don't be afraid to switch camera, particularly when you're doing something that's a little bit less conventional like license of adhesions. or decortications. The beautiful thing about the XI is you can put the camera in any port.
The surgeon performs an inferior to superior dissection, carefully separating the lung tissue from the left internal mammary artery (LIMA), starting from the lower regions.

The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining adhesions between the lung and the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) near the chest wall, focusing on the area where the LIMA emerges from the chest wall.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments under ten times magnification to precisely dissect adhesions away from the lung, demonstrating close proximity to the tissue during the procedure.

I mean, I think this is just such an easier dissection than you can ever do with VATS, no matter how skilled a VATS surgeon you are. When I'm doing a Lima dissection, I tell my anesthesiologist for planning anesthesia, I add forty-five minutes to the case.

But, um, but just be patient and do a good, uh, safe dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects away from the lung parenchyma to prevent air leaks, using two robotic arms while positioning the camera in arm three for a lateral view. This setup provides a better angle for visualizing the lemograph during the procedure.

The surgeon utilizes a fourth instrument, adding a tip-up in arm one, while controlling two instruments with the left hand in arms one and two. The camera is positioned in arm three, and a long bipolar grasper is used in arm four. This setup will change for the lobectomy, transitioning to a conventional approach with the camera in arm two, the cautery in the non-dominant hand in arm one, and the bipolar instrument in the dominant hand in arm three.
But again, I think I get a better look at the at the medial chest with the camera more lateral.

The surgeon begins to visualize the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) with surgical clips visible on it while dissecting the left upper lobe away from the artery. The bipolar instrument is set at eight, ensuring precise energy application between the tips. The lung parenchyma is carefully dissected off the LIMA graft, with a sponge roll used to gently push on the lung for better exposure. The camera is positioned close to the surgical field to enhance visibility.
The surgeon uses two sponge rolls to perform blunt dissection and bipolar cautery, separating the left upper lobe from the chest wall. The lemur is anticipated to be visible soon.

The surgeon carefully dissects the left upper lobe away from the Lima, revealing surgical clips on the Lima. The pericardium is slightly exposed during the process. The final adhesion is released, freeing the lung from the Lima, allowing the surgeon to proceed with the left upper lobectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining adhesions near the pericardium, revealing the superior pulmonary vein. The camera is switched to arm two for a clearer view of the anatomical structures.

Using the conventional camera port, the surgeon operates with the Cartier in the non-dominant hand (arm one), the long bipolar in the dominant hand (arm three), and the tip-up in arm four. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided, revealing lymph nodes near the costochondral sulcus. The division extends up to the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing for further dissection of the posterior hilum.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and expose the posterior hilum, revealing the inferior pulmonary vein as the lowest structure. Above it, the left lower lobe bronchus and the interlobar pulmonary artery are visible. The surgeon also targets the posterior mediastinal lymph nodes, including station seven and subcarinal nodes, creating a landing zone for future stapler use.

The surgeon's assistant holds the lung medially while the surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to push on the superior segment with a sponge roll to avoid injuring the left lower lobe and prevent air leaks. The zero-degree camera provides a view of the dissection of the posterior hilum.

The surgeon attempts to mobilize the lung by lengthening the hilum, addressing potential tethering caused by adhesions. The lung appears less mobile, requiring careful manipulation to improve retraction.

The surgeon separates the inferior pulmonary vein from the superior pulmonary vein, dissecting a hilar lymph node located between them. The area appears slightly bloody as the dissection progresses to lengthen the hilum and increase lung mobility.

The surgeon carefully dissects the superior pulmonary vein away from the left upper lobe bronchus, encountering some bleeding while accessing the left-sided hilar lymph nodes. The truncus anterior branch of the left pulmonary artery becomes visible during the dissection. A vessel loop is placed around the superior pulmonary vein for identification, but it is not divided at this stage.

The surgeon identifies the lingular branch of the superior pulmonary vein, ensuring all branches are exposed. The wide segmental branches of the vein are visible, and the surgeon uses a long curved bipolar instrument to maneuver around them. The preference is to divide the pulmonary artery branches before the veins to minimize vascular congestion and bleeding during fissure dissection.

The surgeon has dissected the superior, anterior, and posterior hilum, encircling it completely. The focus shifts to examining the fissure, attempting to identify the plane for dissection to locate the pulmonary artery, although the yellow hue of the artery is not yet visible.

Other surgeons, particularly surf, he likes to talk about fissure diving, which I do less... The more lobectomies I do, the less fissure diving I do because I think it creates air leaks. Even though I use bipolar on the highest setting, I think if I fissure dive, I'm going to get more air leaks. The chest tubes are going to have to be longer. Patients stay in the hospital longer. So I can tell you that already I'm becoming... sort of pessimistic that I'm going to be able to find the pulmonary artery in that thick fissure. So what I decided to do is let's maybe, again, do what's easy. I've got all, I've got the entire superior pulmonary vein, isolated, all these three segmental branches. So I use my Rob Nell, that's a fourteen French PVC catheter, also known as the Rob Nell. Don't use the Red Robinson, that's made of silastic, it has more friction.

The surgeon uses a Rob Nell catheter to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, preparing to divide it with a surgical stapler. The stapler, loaded with either a gray or white cartridge, is positioned to transect the vein.

The surgeon uses a gray load stapler on the pulmonary artery, guided by a red rubber catheter encircling the three segmental branches. The stapler is articulated and clamped across the artery, despite the challenging angle.

The surgeon considers performing a front-to-back dissection, similar to a VATS lobectomy approach, due to the patient's thick fissure. This approach allows for flexible manipulation of the lobe based on anatomical challenges, aiming to avoid creating large air leaks by not dissecting directly in the fissure.

The surgeon identifies the truncus anterior and the left upper lobe bronchus, dissecting the underside of the truncus anterior away from the bronchus. A lymph node is often present in this area. The Rob Nell catheter is used to find and dilate a small hole, protecting the arch of the aorta on the other side.

The surgeon prepares to take the truncus anterior using a stapler with a gray load, positioned through the posterior or lateral staple port. The superior pulmonary vein was previously taken from the same port, demonstrating the strategic placement of two twelve-millimeter ports for upper lobectomy procedures. The stapler articulates to achieve a straight shot across the truncus anterior.
The surgeon isolates the left upper lobe bronchus, revealing a large lymph node situated between the upper and lower lobe bronchi on the left side, identified as a station X lymph node.

The surgeon identifies and excises the station ten lymph node located between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. The bronchus is visible as the surgeon carefully maneuvers around its backside, with the pulmonary artery branch situated on the opposite side.

I mean, I have nice assistants, they're good, but even if you don't have a great assistant, you know, somebody can put a sucker in there and help you a little bit, retract a little bit.

The surgeon manipulates the left upper lobe bronchus, with the pulmonary artery visibly curling around behind it. Using a tip-up instrument, the surgeon carefully pushes on the bronchus while retracting the lung on either side with a fourth arm. The second tip-up instrument, characterized by its long, blunt, sweeping curve, is employed to safely navigate around the bronchus and adjacent pulmonary vein.

The surgeon is positioned around the bronchus and pulls a red rubber catheter retrograde, ensuring there is enough space for the stapler. The pulmonary artery is visible just to the right and behind the bronchus, with the catheter placed in a safe plane for firing.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the left upper lobe bronchus, ensuring a tight fit across the bronchus. The anesthesiologist is instructed to avoid having a suction catheter in the airway during stapling to prevent accidental stapling of the catheter.

The surgeon has divided the bronchus, revealing the pulmonary artery. The surgeon inspects for additional branches, noting the potential presence of one to three branches. For extremely tiny pulmonary artery branches, the surgeon opts to use small metal clips instead of large plastic clips.
The surgeon pulls the red rubber catheter retrograde around a sizable pulmonary artery branch, ensuring enough space to position the stapler across the branch in the tight surgical field.

This isn't my preference. My preference is to open the major fissure and maybe get the lingular branch and maybe the posterior ascending branch in the fissure. I just think the case goes smoother that way, and you don't have to fire that blind, you know, around the left upper lobronchus, that blind pass.

The surgeon uses a fourth arm to retract the lung upwards and out of the way while preparing for another staple fire. The bronchus is clamped without inflating the lung, maintaining clear anatomical orientation during the dissection.

The surgeon uses a stapler to fire one more staple, aiming to minimize the number of staple fires by taking small branches closer to the hilum with a single staple. The surgical instruments are being cleared from the field.

The surgeon uses an extra-large purple anchor bag to collect tissue, carefully pulling the bag towards the feet to ensure it opens properly. The bag is inserted away from the hilum to avoid injury, while the surgeon completes the lymph node dissection in the AP window area.

The surgeon packs the surgical area with Surgicel, a hemostatic agent, indicating the completion of lymph node dissection across various stations. The Surgicel appears as a cotton-like material used to control bleeding.

The surgeon applies a TACOSIL fibrin sealant patch directly onto the staple line of the lung parenchyma, ensuring it is wet to adhere effectively. The patch is left in place for three minutes to prevent air leaks. Concurrently, a 21-gauge Wang needle is used to inject bupivacaine into the intercostal spaces for a supplemental local block.

So that's a left upper lobectomy. It's a challenging one, but it's definitely one that is doable. And if you have any questions as always, please reach out to me through the contact information on my website. Thanks for watching.
